title: Changing media
description: Practice your skills in this exercise on changing media.

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the chart and answer the question below. 

![Chart titled "Fox news dominated as man campaign news source for Trump voters; no single source as pronounced among Clinton voters." % of voters who named a media outlet as their "main source" for news about the 2016 campaign. The results: Among all voters,  19% reported Fox News was their main source, 13% reported CNN, 8% reported Facebook, 7% reported local TV, 5% reported NBC, 5% reported MSNBC, 5% voted ABC, 4% reported NPR, 4% reported CBS, 3% reported the New York Times, and 3% reported local newspapers.  Among Trump voters, 40% reported Fox News as their main source, 8% reported CNN, 7% reported Facebook, 6% reported NBC, 5% reported local TV, 3% reported ABC, 3% reported CBS, and 3% reported local radio. 
 Among Clinton voters, 18% reported CNN as their main source, 9% reported MSNBC, 8% reported Facebook, 8% reported local TV, 7% reported NPR, 6% reported ABC, 5% reported New Yorkk Times, 5% reported CBS, 4% reported NBC, 4% reported local newspapers, and 3% reported Fox News.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ec3954c8f3aac829a914dd7a383ec6cc.png)
Source: [Pew Research Center](http://www.journalism.org/2017/01/18/trump-clinton-voters-divided-in-their-main-source-for-election-news/) 

**Which of the following is an accurate conclusion based on a comparison of the trends in the chart and your knowledge of political beliefs and behavior?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Media coverage and commentary on political events influence political beliefs and behavior
Media choices have little correlation with political beliefs and behavior
Voters who watch cable news are less likely to participate in the political process than voters who read newspapers
Viewers of ideologically-oriented programs are less informed about political events than viewers of mainstream media

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios is an example of how the media influences political participation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: After watching a debate between experts about a bill up for a vote in the House, Josie calls her senator to ask her to vote against the bill if it makes it to the Senate.
As Lizzie scrolls through her Instagram activity feed, she sees a political advertisement for a candidate in the 2020 presidential election.
While attending a Black Lives Matter protest, Rafael marks his location and posts a status encouraging people to vote in the next local sheriff election.
Landon tunes into a political satire program to watch the host interview his favorite celebrity about their new television show about the White House.

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the infographic and answer the question below.

![Infographic titled "Media Consolidation." Subtitle: "Six companies now control 90% of American media." Panel 1 claims that in 1983, 90% of American media was controlled by 50 companies. Panel 2 claims that as of 2011, just 6 companies control 90% of American media. Those companies are Viacom, CBS, Time Warner, News Corp, Disney, and Comcast.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7913010c45af865219f4ad258f645f3f.png)
Source: [Business Insider](https://www.businessinsider.com/these-6-corporations-control-90-of-the-media-in-america-2012-6) 

**The data displayed in the infographic best supports which of the following statements?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Since 1983, American news sources have become concentrated in the hands of a few companies
Since 1983, American news sources have become increasingly diverse and partisan
As of 2011, Disney and Viacom control 30% of the American media market
As of 2011, all of American media is under the control of six companies

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the infographic and answer the question below.

![Infographic titled "Media Consolidation." Subtitle: "Six companies now control 90% of American media." Panel 1 claims that in 1983, 90% of American media was controlled by 50 companies. Panel 2 claims that as of 2011, just 6 companies control 90% of American media. Those companies are Viacom, CBS, Time Warner, News Corp, Disney, and Comcast.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7913010c45af865219f4ad258f645f3f.png)
Source: [Business Insider](https://www.businessinsider.com/these-6-corporations-control-90-of-the-media-in-america-2012-6) 

**Which of the following is a potential consequence of the change illustrated in the infographic?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The range of viewpoints expressed in news coverage has diminished
The range of viewpoints expressed in news coverage has increased
Large media outlets have started splitting into smaller companies
News segments have started focusing more on hard issues rather than “infotainment”

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the chart and answer the question below. 

![Chart titled "Fox news dominated as man campaign news source for Trump voters; no single source as pronounced among Clinton voters." % of voters who named a media outlet as their "main source" for news about the 2016 campaign. The results: Among all voters,  19% reported Fox News was their main source, 13% reported CNN, 8% reported Facebook, 7% reported local TV, 5% reported NBC, 5% reported MSNBC, 5% voted ABC, 4% reported NPR, 4% reported CBS, 3% reported the New York Times, and 3% reported local newspapers.  Among Trump voters, 40% reported Fox News as their main source, 8% reported CNN, 7% reported Facebook, 6% reported NBC, 5% reported local TV, 3% reported ABC, 3% reported CBS, and 3% reported local radio. 
 Among Clinton voters, 18% reported CNN as their main source, 9% reported MSNBC, 8% reported Facebook, 8% reported local TV, 7% reported NPR, 6% reported ABC, 5% reported New Yorkk Times, 5% reported CBS, 4% reported NBC, 4% reported local newspapers, and 3% reported Fox News.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ec3954c8f3aac829a914dd7a383ec6cc.png)
Source: [Pew Research Center](http://www.journalism.org/2017/01/18/trump-clinton-voters-divided-in-their-main-source-for-election-news/) 

**Which of the following is an accurate statement about the information shown in the chart?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Trump and Clinton voters consumed most of their campaign news from different sources
Facebook was a growing source of news for voters of both parties in 2016 compared to earlier elections
Newspapers and radio stations outperformed TV cable news as the main source of news for voters
Both Trump and Clinton voters were more likely to rely on local news sources rather than national news sources

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the chart and answer the question below. 

![Chart titled "About a third say they often see made-up political news online; 51% say they see inaccurate news." Subtitle: % of US adults who often/sometimes/hardly ever or never come across news online that is completely made up or not fully accurate. The responses were: 32% of respondents said they often see news that is completely made up, 39% say they sometimes see news that is completely made up, and 26% of respondents say they hardly ever or never see news that is completely made up.  51% say they often see news that is not fully accurate, 27% say they sometimes see news that is not fully accurate, and 19% say they hardly ever/never see news that is not fully accurate.  The source of the data is a survey conducted Dec. 1-4, 2016, in a report titled "Many Americans Believe Fake News is Sowing Confusion." ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/56fb976cbdcbf8a921c1f79b4f8743c5.png)
Source: [Pew Research Center](http://www.pewresearch.org/fact-tank/2017/10/04/key-trends-in-social-and-digital-news-media/)

**Which of the following statements is reflected in the data in the chart?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: More than half of US adults report that they often see inaccurate political news online
Most US adults report that they hardly ever see made up or inaccurate news online
Most US adults report that they believe fake news is sowing confusion online
Most political news online is not fully accurate or completely made up

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the chart and answer the question below. 

![Chart titled "About a third say they often see made-up political news online; 51% say they see inaccurate news." Subtitle: % of US adults who often/sometimes/hardly ever or never come across news online that is completely made up or not fully accurate. The responses were: 32% of respondents said they often see news that is completely made up, 39% say they sometimes see news that is completely made up, and 26% of respondents say they hardly ever or never see news that is completely made up.  51% say they often see news that is not fully accurate, 27% say they sometimes see news that is not fully accurate, and 19% say they hardly ever/never see news that is not fully accurate.  The source of the data is a survey conducted Dec. 1-4, 2016, in a report titled "Many Americans Believe Fake News is Sowing Confusion." ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/56fb976cbdcbf8a921c1f79b4f8743c5.png)
Source: [Pew Research Center](http://www.pewresearch.org/fact-tank/2017/10/04/key-trends-in-social-and-digital-news-media/)

**Which of the following is a potential consequence of the trend in the chart?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Increased uncertainty among consumers over the credibility of news sources and information
Increased political efficacy among millennial and Gen X voters
Decreased partisanship among news consumers and social media users
Decreased social media usage in favor of traditional media sources

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is a consequence of consumer-driven media outlets?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: An increase in sensational stories and attack journalism rather than factual reporting
An increase in government propaganda through official state media channels
An increase in the diversity of viewpoints as media conglomerates splinter into smaller companies
An increase in programming that challenges the existing beliefs of partisan viewers

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following provides the most accurate explanation of how increased media choices have affected partisan polarization?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It gives people several options of where they get their news from, leading them to choose media that aligns with their ideological beliefs.
It has reduced the number of people who identify with a political ideology, leading to a rise in the number of moderate voters.
It has forced news providers to focus on staying moderate, otherwise they may lose consumers to organizations they see as unbiased.
It allows for the constant streaming of political news, leading to a more informed and politically engaged electorate.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements best describes the impact of ideologically-oriented programming?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: People are more likely to choose a program that matches their ideology, filtering the news they receive.
People are more likely to avoid ideologically-oriented media sources as they are often biased sources of news.
People are more likely to consume a wide-range of media sources to keep themselves as impartial as possible.
People are more likely to avoid paying attention to the news, as they are unsure about the credibility of the reporting.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below. 

>“We advocate abolishing the term "citizen journalist." These people can call themselves "citizen news gatherers," but it is no more appropriate to call them citizen journalists than it would be to sit before a citizen judge or be operated on by a citizen brain surgeon.
 
Because of declining revenues, newspapers, magazines and TV stations actually think they can get these "volunteers" to replace the professionals . . . that they can abandon their print editions, and have bloggers post without covering stories as they happen. Yes, there are a million news blogs, but only one New York Times. But to continue its reputation, the Times must uphold traditional editorial principles. So must we all.” 
 
-Editorial, [The Digital Journalist]((http://digitaljournalist.org/issue0912/lets-abolish-citizen-journalists.html), 2009 

**Based on the text, which of the following statements would the author most likely agree with?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Although bloggers can cover stories as they happen, they do not have the training to provide good quality news coverage
The vast number of news blogs has made traditional publications like the New York Times unnecessary
Traditional editorial principles are needlessly restrictive in the digital age
It is inaccurate to label citizen journalists “volunteers” as they are usually paid by newspapers and TV stations for their stories

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is a true statement about the role media plays in providing citizens with political knowledge?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It enhances people’s access to political information so they can make thoughtful decisions about their representatives and policy.
It can determine a citizen's core political beliefs about the role of the government in the United States.
It can impact the outcome of an election as their reporting can encourage or discourage citizens to vote.
It holds the different levels of government accountable for their actions by acting as a watchdog.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement best describes an effect of ideologically oriented news outlets in a democracy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Citizens may become more polarized due to exposure to like-minded viewpoints.
Citizens are more likely to engage with balanced perspectives.
News organizations reduce their partisan rhetoric to attract a broader audience base.
Politicians are less likely to tailor their messages to specific audiences.

assessment_items.question_content:
A recent college graduate primarily consumes political content through a satirical YouTube channel. The channel’s creators prioritize increasing likes, shares, and subscriptions by using humor and dramatic commentary to mock partisan cable news. The graduate frequently reposts clips and avoids traditional journalism, claiming it is “boring and out of touch.”

**What does the scenario reveal about how different media can affect political engagement?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It shows how political entertainment can influence beliefs by emphasizing attention over accuracy.
It shows how traditional news outlets strengthen democracy by providing accurate and balanced information.
It shows how large media companies can reduce engagement by prioritizing consolidation over diversity.
It shows how public media builds trust and engagement by offering nonpartisan political information.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the most likely effect of widespread uncertainty about the credibility of news sources?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Some voters become less engaged in politics and may choose not to vote.
More citizens begin fact-checking political information using academic research databases.
Public trust in the media grows as people seek out a wider range of news outlets.
Political campaigns reduce their use of social media to avoid spreading misinformation.
